parameters.name: Depth to top of massive ice, Arcadia-class mid-latitude site
parameters.source: SWIM refined maps (Morgan et al. 2025, PSJ 6:37) 0-1 m consistency band; Dundas et al. 2018 Science 359:199 scarps show ice 1-2 m below surface
parameters.notes: Highly site-variable at sub-km scale; decimeters in best Arcadia/Utopia sites (expanded secondary craters), 1-2 m at Dundas scarps. Treat as site parameter; conservative designs (RedWater) assume up to 20 m overburden.

parameters.name: Thickness of excess-ice layer, Arcadia Planitia
parameters.source: Bramson et al. 2015 GRL 42:6566 (SHARAD + crater morphology): depth-to-base mode 42 m, mean 51 +/- 18 m; Dundas 2018 scarps expose >100 m in places
parameters.notes: Volume ~1e4 km3 in Arcadia alone; effectively unlimited reservoir for settlement-scale demand (600 t/Starship = ~650 m3 of ice).

parameters.name: Ice mass fraction of mid-latitude massive ice deposits
parameters.source: Dundas et al. 2018 (massive, dust-poor scarp ice); Bramson et al. 2015 (bulk dielectric ~2.5, mostly clean ice); M-WIP 2016 reference Case A assumes 90% ice / 10% sand
parameters.notes: No in-situ ground truth yet. Purity mainly drives Rodwell sediment/sump management and melt-water dirtiness, not extraction energy (weak function above ~0.5).

parameters.name: Water content of pure gypsum (CaSO4.2H2O)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry (2x18.02/172.17); dehydration at 100-150 C confirmed in M-WIP 2016
parameters.notes: Releases water at benign 100-150 C (vs 300 C for smectite) - lowest-energy regolith route. Gypsum/polyhydrated-sulfate deposits mapped from orbit (e.g., north polar erg, Valles Marineris, Gale).

parameters.name: Bulk recoverable water fraction, gypsum-enriched regolith deposit
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 reference Case B: 40% gypsum + minor hydrates -> 186 t ore per 16 t H2O at 425 K
parameters.notes: Hypothetical reserve description, +/-30% heterogeneity assumed; no gypsum deposit has been ground-truthed at mining scale.

parameters.name: Bulk recoverable water fraction, smectite clay-enriched deposit
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 reference Case C: 40% smectite (avg 4 wt% water; Na-smectite ~2, Ca-smectite ~7) -> 584 t ore per 16 t H2O at 575 K
parameters.notes: Requires ~300 C process temperature. Phyllosilicates widely mapped by CRISM/OMEGA (3-8 wt% in enriched terrains per hydrated-silicate surveys).

parameters.name: Recoverable water fraction of typical (globally ubiquitous) regolith
parameters.source: MSL DAN/SAM (Gale): 1.5-3 wt% total; Kleinhenz & Paz AIAA 2017-0423: 1.3 wt% recoverable at 300 C; M-WIP Case D
parameters.notes: The 'works anywhere' fallback: 10-100x the excavated mass and 4-8x the thermal energy per kg water vs gypsum ore or ice.

parameters.name: Bulk density of undisturbed granular regolith
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 heuristics: undisturbed granular ~2.0 +/-10% t/m3 (35% porosity); disturbed/excavated ~1.5 +/-10% t/m3 (50% porosity)
parameters.notes: Use 1500 kg/m3 for hauled (disturbed) material volume sizing; 920 kg/m3 for pure ice.

parameters.name: Perchlorate mass fraction in Mars soil
parameters.source: Hecht et al. 2009, Science 325:64 (Phoenix WCL, 0.4-0.6 wt%); corroborated by MSL SAM at Gale
parameters.notes: Mostly Mg/Ca/Na perchlorate + chlorates. Toxic (thyroid) at ppm levels -> all soil-contacted or melt water needs treatment before crew use; electrolyzers also require cleanup. Massive glacial ice expected much cleaner but not measured.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 excavator mass
parameters.source: Mueller et al., RASSOR 2.0, ASCE Earth & Space 2016, NTRS 20210011366
parameters.notes: Counter-rotating bucket drums give near-zero net excavation force (works in 0.38 g). Reference excavator in M-WIP and Kleinhenz & Paz sizing.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 regolith payload per trip
parameters.source: Mueller et al., RASSOR 2.0, ASCE 2016, NTRS 20210011366 (2 x 40 kg drums)
parameters.notes: Payload/vehicle mass ratio 1.2 - unusually high for planetary rovers.

parameters.name: RASSOR 2.0 excavation rate (minimum demonstrated design point)
parameters.source: Mueller et al. ASCE 2016: >=2.7 t per 24 h day (=2,770 kg/sol); 0.38 kg vehicle mass per (kg/h) rate
parameters.notes: Earth prototype in simulant; apply 60% duty cycle (M-WIP battery/recharge assumption) for fleet sizing. One RASSOR moves ~1.8 t/sol of ore at 60% duty.

parameters.name: Specific excavation energy, granular regolith (RASSOR-class)
parameters.source: Mueller et al. ASCE 2016: 4 W per (kg/h) excavation rate = 4 Wh/kg
parameters.notes: Negligible vs extraction heat: at 8.6 wt% yield ~0.05 kWh/kg water, at 1.3 wt% ~0.31 kWh/kg water. Icy/ice-cemented ground is NOT excavatable by RASSOR - needs drilling (Rodwell) or aggressive cutters; use upper range +50-100% for duricrust.

parameters.name: Excavator traverse speed
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 RASSOR key characteristics (25 cm/s)
parameters.notes: Drives trips/sol = f(haul distance); at 1 km one-way a round trip is ~2.2 h + load/dump.

parameters.name: Practical max ore-haul distance, single-digit excavator fleet
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 excavation/travel analysis: gypsum case feasible to ~3 km with 2 RASSOR-class excavators in 480-sol campaign; typical regolith excavator-limited even at 100 m
parameters.notes: Design rule: co-locate plant and mine; haul or pump water, not low-grade ore.

parameters.name: Surface transport specific energy (regolith/water hauling)
parameters.source: Estimate: rolling resistance physics, Crr 0.15-0.25 loose regolith, g=3.71 m/s2, drivetrain efficiency ~0.5; consistent with M-WIP RASSOR traverse assumptions
parameters.notes: E = Crr*g*d/eta per kg hauled. Slow rovers with high hotel loads (MSL-style) can be 5x worse; purpose-built haulers approach the physics limit.

parameters.name: Theoretical energy to melt massive ice (from -80 C)
parameters.source: Thermodynamics: cp_ice ~1.9 kJ/kg.K avg x 80 K + 334 kJ/kg fusion = ~486 kJ/kg; NASA JSC ice drilling assessment chart ~140 kWh per 1000 kg for -80 C ice
parameters.notes: Lower bound of all routes. -20 C ice: ~0.10 kWh/kg. Rodwell practice runs 3-5x this due to conductive losses into surrounding ice and evaporative losses at the pool surface.

parameters.name: Theoretical energy to sublime ice (vapor-transport extraction routes)
parameters.source: Thermodynamics: L_sub = 2838 kJ/kg + sensible heat from ~210 K = ~2940 kJ/kg
parameters.notes: Applies to open-pit volatilization, auger dryers venting vapor, and any route that boils/evaporates rather than melts. Condenser heat (~2.5 MJ/kg) is recoverable in principle (recuperation is the big design lever).

parameters.name: Practical thermal energy to extract water from icy regolith (20-40 wt% ice, sublimation/vapor route incl. matrix heating)
parameters.source: Thermo model ((1-y)/y*c_reg*dT + c_ice*dT + L_sub) bracketed by NASA ISRU studies (Kleinhenz NTRS 20180005542 program targets; Hoffman JSC assessment); lower bound assumes partial heat recuperation
parameters.notes: Lab capture efficiencies remain poor at low TRL (Linne & Kleinhenz 2016 breadboard: 17% of heater energy into soil; LUWEX lunar demo ~1-2% overall). Multiply by 1.2-2.0 for near-term systems.

parameters.name: Mars Rodwell water production at 10 kW thermal (CRREL simulation, -80 C ice)
parameters.source: Hoffman et al.: CRREL Mars sims 120-400 L/day at 2-10 kW(th); 100 gal/day (379 kg/day) balances at ~10 kW. NASA/TP-20205011353 & JSC ice drilling assessment
parameters.notes: Scales ~linearly with thermal power in this range (see specific energy param). Withdrawal > melt rate collapses (refreezes) the well; overpowering wastes heat growing an unused pool. Wells restartable after deliberate freeze-down (South Pole/Camp Century heritage). Antarctic anchors: South Pole station well ~2,005 t/yr; IceCube wells ~1,140 t/season.

parameters.name: Rodwell steady-state specific thermal energy (Mars, -80 C ice)
parameters.source: Derived from Hoffman/CRREL simulation points: 2 kW/120 L/day = 0.40; 10 kW/379-400 L/day = 0.60-0.63 kWh/kg (NASA/TP-20205011353; JSC ice-mining assessment 2016)
parameters.notes: THERMAL, not electric - can be reactor waste heat. Excludes drilling, pumping (mgh ~0.0002 kWh/kg per 150 m lift - negligible), cleanup, and startup transient (~10-30 sols at full power before first water; restart cost similar). Warmer mid-latitude ice (210-230 K at depth) cuts this 20-40%. Borehole must be sealed/pressurized >611 Pa to suppress evaporation/boiling.

parameters.name: Design borehole depth for Mars Rodwell through overburden into ice
parameters.source: RedWater design envelope: drill through up to ~20-25 m overburden (Mellerowicz et al. 2022, New Space, doi:10.1089/space.2021.0057); pool cavity forms 2.5-5 m tall, 5-10 m dia below that (Hoffman CRREL sims)
parameters.notes: Well migrates downward as ice is consumed; South Pole wells abandoned at ~150 m pump-lift depth after ~a decade. For sim: new borehole per ~10^3-10^4 t withdrawn or model lift growth.

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, gypsum-enriched ore (8.6 wt%, 425 K)
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 Case B: ~2 kW for 33 kg H2O/sol; theory floor ~1.15 kWh/kg (matrix heating + dehydration enthalpy)
parameters.notes: Cheapest regolith route and lowest process temperature (100-150 C) - also lowest corrosion risk (no HCl/H2S evolution below ~400 C).

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, smectite ore (2.7 wt%, 575 K)
parameters.source: M-WIP 2016 Case C: ~5 kW for 33 kg H2O/sol; theory floor ~3.1 kWh/kg
parameters.notes: Dominated by heating 36 kg of matrix per kg of water to 300 C; recuperator effectiveness is the key design lever.

parameters.name: Soil-processing thermal energy per kg water, typical regolith (1.3 wt%, 575 K)
parameters.source: Range spans M-WIP Case D2 (~8 kW/33 kg/sol = 5.9) and Kleinhenz & Paz AIAA 2017-0423 (17 kW thermal for 1.36 kg/h = 12.5); disagreement from recuperation and yield assumptions
parameters.notes: Sources genuinely disagree 2x - carry the range, not the mean. Kleinhenz & Paz full-system context: 52 kW / 1.7 t total for MAV-scale plant; heat could come from fission waste heat, collapsing the electric penalty.

parameters.name: TRIDENT 1-m-class rotary-percussive drill mass
parameters.source: Honeybee Robotics/NASA (VIPER, PRIME-1): <20 kg, rotary and percussive actuators 200 W each, 200 N max weight-on-bit (Zacny et al., PSJ doi:10.3847/PSJ/ae0b51; Glass et al. 2024, NTRS 20240000585)
parameters.notes: Bite-sampling in 10 cm (down to 2 cm in hard material) increments. Flown hardware class (PRIME-1/IM-2, 2025). Scale reference for prospecting and pilot-hole drilling; RedWater coiled-tube drill is the production-scale analog.

parameters.name: Drilling energy per meter of borehole (regolith to ice-cemented ground)
parameters.source: Estimate: TRIDENT actuator power (~100-400 W draw) at field penetration rates - loose soil ~1+ m/h, massive ice-cemented material as slow as 0.16 m/h (7 cm/27 min, Haughton Crater, Glass et al. 2024, NTRS 20240000585)
parameters.notes: Energy trivial vs extraction; time and bit-life dominate. For sim: drilling rate parameter matters more than energy - use 0.15-1 m/h in ice-cemented ground.

parameters.name: Water cleanup energy (filtration + perchlorate removal + deionization, electrolysis-grade)
parameters.source: Estimate: terrestrial ion-exchange/biocatalytic perchlorate removal is <0.01 kWh/kg but consumable-limited; vapor-compression distillation bounds the robust route at ~0.1-0.2 kWh/kg
parameters.notes: No integrated Mars-water treatment demo has published end-to-end energy. Regolith-dryer condensate dirtier than Rodwell melt (volatilized Cl/S species above ~400 C). Perchlorate is also an O2 resource (brine electrolysis concepts) but no baseline credits it.

parameters.name: Water consumed per kg methane produced (Sabatier + electrolysis, net)
parameters.source: Stoichiometry: net 2 H2O + CO2 -> CH4 + 2 O2; 2x18.02/16.04 = 2.246. Cross-check: Kleinhenz & Paz require 15,701 kg H2O for 6,978 kg CH4 (ratio 2.25)
parameters.notes: Co-produces 3.99 kg O2 per kg CH4 - more than Raptor's ~3.5-3.8 O/F needs, so no additional water is required for oxidizer in an all-Sabatier plant (~14% O2 surplus for life support/losses).

parameters.name: Water required to fully refuel one Starship on Mars
parameters.source: SpaceX Starship Block 2/V3 specs: ~1,500-1,600 t ship propellant, ~330-350 t CH4 (en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SpaceX_Starship_(spacecraft); payloadspace.com/what-to-expect-from-starship-v3) x 2.246 kg H2O/kg CH4
parameters.notes: Range covers propellant loads 1,100-1,500 t across Starship versions and O/F 3.5-3.8. Over one synodic period (~640 sols) this is ~940 kg/sol = 2.4 Rodwells at 10 kW(th) each, or ~24 kW(th) continuous at 0.6 kWh/kg - water extraction is the logistics driver of propellant ISRU, not the energy driver (electrolysis/liquefaction dominate energy). verified correction: prior 600 t anchored to the superseded V1 1,200 t load; flying Block 2/V3 (1,500-1,600 t, ~330-350 t CH4) implies ~740-790 t water/reload, above the old 660 t max. Downstream per-sol rates (~940 kg/sol -> ~1,160 kg/sol) are ~25% higher.

modeling_notes: L0 (scalar): water_rate [kg/sol] = P_thermal x 24.66 / e_specific, with e_specific = 0.6 kWh(th)/kg (Rodwell/massive ice), 1.5 (gypsum ore), 3.7 (smectite), 6-12.5 (typical regolith); add 0.1 kWh(e)/kg cleanup, 0.004/yield kWh/kg excavation for regolith routes, 0.3 kWh/t-km hauling; apply 0.6-0.8 availability. L1 (analytic daily/seasonal): ice temperature annual wave T(z,t) with skin depth ~1-2 m sets sensible-heat term; Rodwell as two-state system (startup: drill + 10-30 sols pool establishment; steady: max withdrawal = eta*P/(c_ice*dT + L_fus), collapse if exceeded; downward migration grows pump lift; abandon/redrill at ~150 m-equivalent); excavator fleet sizing N = ceil(ore_rate/(2770 kg/sol x 0.6 duty)) with traverse kinematics at 0.25 m/s and 80 kg loads; credit reactor waste heat against thermal loads on nuclear branches. L2 (sub-sol physics): CRREL-type lumped pool energy balance dT_pool/dt with melt flux from Nu = C*Ra^(1/3), conduction into ice annulus of radius ~4.5 r_pool (Lunardini & Rand 1995), and Mars-specific evaporative loss Sh = C'*Ra^(1/3) suppressed by borehole pressurization >611 Pa (JSC bell-jar experiments, NASA/TP-20205011353); regolith dryer as plug-flow ore heating with condenser recovery and recuperator effectiveness 0.3-0.7 (the lever explaining the 5.9 vs 12.5 kWh/kg disagreement between M-WIP and Kleinhenz & Paz); Monte-Carlo ore grade +/-30% (M-WIP heterogeneity). Key cautions: all Rodwell Mars numbers are simulation-based for -80 C ice (no Mars-environment full-scale demo; TRL 6 from Antarctic heritage); lab thermal-capture efficiencies at low TRL are 5-50x worse than system studies assume; SpaceX figures are company claims - parameterize per-ship reload.
key_findings: Massive, ~90%-pure excess ice is confirmed at decimeters-to-2-m depth across Arcadia/Utopia Planitia (SWIM maps, Dundas 2018 scarps, Bramson 2015 SHARAD): at a good site the reservoir is effectively unlimited (10^4 km3 in Arcadia alone) and the binding constraints are access depth, energy per kg, and equipment duty cycle - not resource exhaustion.

The Rodwell is the standout bulk-water technique: 60+ years of Antarctic/Greenland operational heritage (South Pole station draws ~2,005 t/yr; IceCube wells made ~1,140 t/season), NASA-judged TRL 6, and CRREL Mars simulations give 120-400 L/day at 2-10 kW thermal, i.e. 0.4-0.7 kWh(th)/kg - within 3-5x of the 0.135 kWh/kg melting-theory floor, and the heat can be reactor waste heat.

Regolith bound water costs 2.5-20x more energy than ice per kg of water (gypsum ore ~1.5, smectite ~3.7, typical regolith 5.9-12.5 kWh(th)/kg) and 10-100x more excavated mass, but works at any latitude; gypsum-enriched deposits (8.6 wt%, released at only 100-150 C) are the only regolith route competitive for propellant-scale production.

Excavation and drilling energy are negligible (RASSOR: 0.004 kWh/kg regolith; drilling: ~0.05-2 kWh/m) - the real excavation-side constraints are fleet size, haul distance (M-WIP: ore hauling impractical beyond ~3 km), duty cycle, and drilling rate in ice-cemented ground (as slow as 0.16 m/h in TRIDENT field tests).

One full Starship reload (~1,200 t propellant, company claim) requires ~600 t of water via net stoichiometry 2 H2O + CO2 -> CH4 + 2 O2 (2.246 kg H2O per kg CH4); the co-produced O2 exceeds Raptor's mixture-ratio needs, so no extra water is needed for oxidizer. That is ~940 kg/sol over one synodic period - about 2.4 ten-kW Rodwells - confirming water supply is a logistics/reliability problem, while electrolysis/liquefaction dominate the energy problem.

Biggest epistemic gaps: no Mars-environment Rodwell demo (evaporation near the triple point demands borehole pressurization; JSC found pool-surface losses scale as Ra^(1/3)); lab water-capture efficiencies 5-50x below system-study assumptions; typical-regolith processing energy disagrees 2x between M-WIP (5.9) and Kleinhenz & Paz (12.5 kWh/kg); Phoenix-measured perchlorate (0.4-0.6 wt% of soil) forces a water-cleanup stage whose end-to-end energy has never been published.
